title: Tuples & Sets
overview: Python offers more ways to store collections. Tuples are ordered and unchangeable; sets are unordered with unique items.

blocks.title: What is a Tuple?
blocks.body: A **tuple** is like a list but **immutable** — you cannot change it after creation. Use parentheses ().
bullets: Items cannot be added, removed, or changed
Tuples are faster than lists and protect data from modification
Use tuples for data that should stay constant

blocks.title: A Tuple is Like a Sealed Envelope
blocks.text: Once sealed with coordinates inside, you can read them but not change them. Tuples work the same — great for fixed data like RGB colors or dates.

blocks.title: Think Of A Tuple As A Sealed Envelope

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Sealed Envelope
leftPanel.description: Once sealed, contents cannot change. Safe and tamper-proof.

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent:
Tuple = Sealed Envelope:
• Create once with ( )
• Contents are fixed
• Cannot add/remove/change

blocks.question: Can you change the first value of coords = (10, 20) by writing coords[0] = 5?
blocks.answer: No! Tuples are immutable — once created, their contents cannot change. Trying coords[0] = 5 would give an error. This is the whole point of a tuple: it guarantees the data stays the same, like a sealed envelope.

blocks.title: Creating and Using Tuples
blocks.code:
colors = (255, 0, 0)
coordinates = (10.5, 20.3)
person = ("Alice", 25, "Engineer")

print(colors[0])
print(coordinates)
print(person[1])

# This would FAIL:
# colors[0] = 128  # TypeError!

blocks.title: Tuple Packing and Unpacking
blocks.body: **Packing** creates a tuple by grouping values. **Unpacking** extracts values into separate variables.
bullets: Swap variables: a, b = b, a (uses tuple unpacking)
Great for returning multiple values from functions

blocks.title: Packing and Unpacking
blocks.code:
point = 10, 20
print(point)

x, y = point
print(f"x = {x}, y = {y}")

a = 5
b = 10
a, b = b, a
print(f"a = {a}, b = {b}")

blocks.title: Work with Tuples
blocks.instructions: Create a tuple with your name, age, and city. Unpack it and print each value.
blocks.solution:
my_info = ("Lakshanya", 20, "Chennai")
name, age, city = my_info
print(f"Name: {name}")
print(f"Age: {age}")
print(f"City: {city}")
blocks.hint: Unpack by putting variable names separated by commas on the left.

blocks.title: What is a Set?
blocks.body: A **set** is an unordered collection of **unique** items. Duplicates are automatically removed.
bullets: Sets have no duplicates — each item appears once
Sets are unordered — cannot access by index
Great for removing duplicates and membership tests

blocks.title: Think Of A Set As A Bag Of Unique Marbles

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Bag Of Unique Items
leftPanel.description: Every marble appears only once. No duplicates allowed.

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent:
Set = Unique Collection:
• No duplicate items
• Unordered (no index)
• Fast membership testing

blocks.question: If you create a set with {1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3}, how many items will it contain?
blocks.answer: The set will contain 3 items: {1, 2, 3}. All duplicates are automatically removed because a set only stores unique values. This is like having a bag of marbles where you toss out any duplicates — only one of each color remains.

blocks.title: Creating Sets
blocks.code:
numbers = {1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 4, 3}
print(numbers)

fruits = {"apple", "banana", "apple", "cherry"}
print(fruits)

my_list = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3]
unique = set(my_list)
print(unique)

blocks.title: Set Operations
blocks.body: Sets support mathematical operations like union, intersection, and difference.
bullets: union (|) — all items from both sets
difference (-) — items in first but NOT second
in — check membership (very fast in sets)

blocks.title: Set Operations
blocks.code:
a = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
b = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8}

print(f"Union: {a | b}")
print(f"Intersection: {a & b}")
print(f"A - B: {a - b}")
print(f"B - A: {b - a}")

print(3 in a)
print(9 in b)

blocks.title: Remove Duplicates
blocks.instructions: Remove duplicates from [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5, 3, 5] using a set, then sort.
blocks.hint: Use set(numbers) to remove duplicates, then sorted() to sort.

keyTakeaways.text: Tuples are immutable ordered collections with ()
Tuple packing groups values; unpacking extracts them
Use tuples for swapping: a, b = b, a

whatYouLearned: How to create and use tuples
How tuple packing and unpacking works
How to swap variables with tuple unpacking
How to create sets and remove duplicates
